name: Ebisu
description: Ebisu, also known as Hiruko, is one of the Seven Gods of Fortune (Shichi Fukujin) in Japanese mythology. He is often depicted with a high, pointed hat, holding a fishing rod and a tai (sea bream), symbolizing abundance and good fortune. Ebisu is the only one of the seven gods believed to have originated purely from Japan, without influence from Indian or Chinese deities. His origins are shrouded in myth, with some accounts claiming he was born deformed and cast out to sea. He eventually washed ashore and, after overcoming his physical disabilities, became a benevolent deity. Ebisu is revered as the god of fishermen, merchants, and prosperity. He is believed to bring good luck in business, safe voyages for sailors, and bountiful catches for fishermen. Shrines dedicated to Ebisu are often located near the sea or in commercial districts. He is often depicted as cheerful and approachable, a symbol of honest labor and fair dealing. Offerings of fish and sake are commonly made to Ebisu to ensure continued blessings of wealth and success. His association with the sea bream is particularly strong, making it a popular offering and a symbol of celebration. Ebisu's enduring popularity reflects the importance of commerce and the sea in Japanese culture.
subtitle: God of Fishermen, Prosperity, and Luck

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Hiruko
keyMyths.description: Ebisu, initially known as Hiruko, was born deformed as the first child of Izanagi and Izanami. Due to his deformities, he was cast adrift in a reed boat.

keyMyths.title: Ebisu's Arrival Ashore
keyMyths.description: After being cast adrift, Hiruko eventually washed ashore and was cared for by the Ainu people. He overcame his disabilities to become a powerful deity.

keyMyths.title: Ebisu and the Sea Bream
keyMyths.description: Ebisu is always depicted with a sea bream, which symbolizes good fortune and abundance. The sea bream is often offered to him at shrines.

sources.author: Donald Philippi
